CURRENT AFFAIRS.

Politics in Ireland are in a pretty tangle. As most
of our own immediate programme concerns the verb
‘“ to do,’’ irrespective of what party is in power in Eng-
land, and irrespective of how those comparatively few
who take a keen interest in politics, pure and simple,
in Treland are wrangling amongst themselves and call-
ing ome another names, we look upon the present mix
up as sadly amused outsiders; for even though we do
not believe very much in the Parliamentarians and do
not take Mr. Martyn, of the Kildare Street Club,
seriously, we are sorry to see Irishmen making a sort of
political holiday for Englishmen and England’s Faith-
ful Garrison in Ireland by giving a display of barging
and bickering. The real work to be done in the living
present, despite our political disabilities, is being partly
overlooked, and it is being partly done by people who
are sick alike of the Parliamentary Party and of the

antics of Edward Martyn and Co.

It is easier to come to the front in what stands for
the political world in Ireland—for there is not much
skilled competition in the business—than it is to excel
as a manufacturer, a professional man, or an up-to-date
business man. An Empee or an anti-Empee does
not require as much skill as a creamery manager, a

T

skilled farmer, or a factory foreman, so there are
plenty of spouting politicians in the market—a market
from which intellect and capacity tend to be driven
just as capital is shy and tends to keep away when
things arc unsettled and uncertain. Mr. C. J. Dolan,
M.P., intends to resign the happy state of being an
Empee that he may endeavour to establish  his
right to go to Parliament again in order that he may
heroically stay out!

Iere now is Sir Thomas IHenry Grattan Esmonde,
Bart., writing a long letter to a Martynette meeting.
It is a puzzling document. It reads like a mixture of
a weak solution of Henry Grattan’s rhetoric and Sir
T. Esmonde’s own brains. A Justice of the Peace is
anathema in Martynette circles, we understand, and
yet at the Martynette meeting a letter from one of these
““ traitors,”” Mr. J. Donohue, J.P., was read!

Let us take Sir T. Esmonde’s letter.  Here is a
sentence : —‘‘ I am convinced that Parliamentary agita-
tion, as now conducted, has spent its force, and that
nothing more can he gained by it on its present lines.”
Why does not Sir T. Esmonde resign then? All signs
would lead the impartial observer to conciude that not
only will a man be found willing to take up the vacant
post, but that there will be a scramble for it. TIf he
remains an Empee, and if the Catholic primary schools
in England—which practically mean the schools of the
Trish exiles and their descendants—are threatened, will
he tell Cardinal Logue that ‘‘ Parliamentary agitation,
as now conducted, has spent its force.”” Perhaps, he
will, but before we express an opinion on the point
we will wait until the occasion arises.

Sir Thomas is not at one with the Martynettes in
that he says that Parliamentary agitation has gained
much for Ireland in the past; the Martynettes, we
understand, maintain the contrary. Sir Thomas is a
sort of political convert, and his conversion has come
about, it would seem, not so much as a consequence of
ripening thought as from baffled hopes and unsound
judgment. He has looked for the treasure in one part
of the field for 22 years, confident all the time that he
was looking for it that it was to be found there. “Now
he has concluded that it is not to be found in that
spot. What faith are intelligent people to have in him
should he tell them, after 22 years’ search, {o go look
in another part of the field. ITe says:—-‘We have
supported the English Liberals in the confident ex-
pectation that, when their day came, they would con-
cede legislative and administrative autonomy to Ire-
land.  Our hopes have been rudely disappointed.’”
Perhaps, if Sir Thomas turns his hopes from the floor
of the House to Napoleon, who has just forced his way
by virtue of British Law and quibbles on to the floor of
the Kildare Street Club, he may in another 22 years
find that his hopes ‘‘ have been rudely disappointed.’
Sir T. Esmonde is a very reputable man, and we belicve
a thoroughly well intentioned Irishman, but we:do nos
know that he has ever shown much traces of Henry
Grattan, except in his name.

Sir Thomas says: —‘‘ Personally, I rejoice that this
situation has arisen, it clears our mind of cant; . . .»”
We are not so sure about the cant. For in this letter
where Sir T. Esmonde announces, by implication, that

.his mind has been cleared of cant, there is a paragraph

which, in our opinion, is the quintessence of cant.
Hero it is: —‘“ Ireland is no Colony. She is no new
crezticn of* Municipal fancy. Ireland is a sovereign
nation, with a civilisation more ancient than that of
England, with Parliamentary institutions far older
than hers. Why, then, should we continue to beg with

hated breath and whispering humbleness in a foreign
Legislature, from a foreign people, for the restoration
of our stolen liberties—liberties which come to us, not
from England, nor from England’s Parliament, but
- from Almighty God!”’ Iere we have the shadow,
but not the substance—imperfect though that sub-
stance was—of Henry Grattan. ‘‘Treland 7s a sover-
eign nation,”” we are assured! Then what the dickens
is all the pother about? She has a civilization more
ancient than Eungland! Then Anglicisation is only
2 delusion and a dream! She has Parliamentary insti-
tutions far older than England! Then why be even
chairman of a General Council of County Councils, a
petty office made possible by a recent Act passed by the
usurping British House of Commons! As Sir Thomas
was having his brains cleared of cant he might have
ordered his valet to rub out the hallucinations.

In one part of his letter Sir Thomas says:— T do
not believe that the English people will ever grant
Home Rule, or anything like it, to Ireland, if they can
help it. Why, then, degrade ourselves by begging for
it?  And why ask our people, who should be a proud
and self-reliant people, to submit to the continuance of
a policy of obsequious expectancy from the liberality of
England?’’ TIn another part he says that we should
abandon the request for Home Rule and revert ““ to our
historic and constitutional demand for the Repeai of
the Act of Union.” And what form, pray, is the
‘‘demand ”’ to take? We find from the Directory
that Sir Thomas was formerly a Licut. in the 6th
Brig., S. Irish Div., R.A. We are not up in Tommy
Atkins’ affairs and contractions, but we take it that all
that means that Sir Thomas was once a militiaman, even
though Ireland is a sovereign nation. Perhaps he has
an old militia gun at home that he will take down from
the rafters when he is making his ‘“ demand ’’ for Re-
peal. By all means, let us clear our minds of cant!

The Martynettes passed resolutions at Ennis-
corthy, where the letters from the ex-militia lieutenant
and the present J.P. were read, and in the course of
these deadly fusilades of resolutions let off under the
shadow of Vinegar Hill, we read:—‘‘ That we regard
the letter of the Chief Whip of the Irish Party, which
we have heard read with pleasure and satisfaction, as
striking testimony from the inside, not only of the
failure of the Parliamentary method but of its complete
breakdown ; that we warmly congratulate Sir Thomas
Esmonde on its very timely and patriotic pronounce-
ment, which for its courage and honesty is in every way
worthy of Zis past career and of the illustrious name he
bears, and that this meeting of his fellow-countrymen
hereby records its appreciation of the lead he has given
to his colleagues, and we cordially pledge him our
renewed confidence and support, and heartily invite
him to take that place in the Sinn Fein movement
which his high talents and eminent position entitle
him to.”

This is a rather mixed fusilade from the base of
Vinegar Hill. ‘ Worthy of his past carcer.” Ts it
- worthy of the time he was in the ‘“millectia”’! He
has not left the Party, he is still a member, and yet
the Martynettes ‘ pledge him our renewed confidence
and support”’! Ts there.not rank shoneenry in the
reference to his ‘‘ eminent position.”” What is eminent
about his position beyond that he is a barcnet.

There was also a meeting in the Pheenix Park, not of
the Martynettes, but of a section who are, we under-

sand e ‘“advanced.”” The name of ‘Charles
SSllqt'::m,r(, ni‘;:rncll was cheered, and quite rightly. But
what did Sir Thomas Esmonde do by Parnell? Was
he a Parnollite? If he forsook Pal‘ncll_and is now ‘ror-
taking Redmond, perhaps he will in time forsake, the
Kildare Stret Club Napoleon.

One of the speakers at the Park said, as reported,
that—‘¢ He did not like to accuse men of mercenary
motives, but he was creditably ix}formec_l that when men
in a public position refused to give their own 'collleagu?s
a balance sheet of how things are managed within their
own party there is something they want to Iu(‘l(c." Th,?
groater Ireland that is conjugating the verb ‘‘to do
in the fields, the factories and workshops and schools
of Treland, and that is more or less, or_altogf:thcr, Sw_k
alike of Parliamentarianism and antx-Pm’hameptarl-
anism, will not be attracted to the arena of pohhcs by
such insinuations of financial swindling and dishonesty.

What immediate prospect is there of the Parliamen-
tary Party getting slation for Ireland? What pro-
spect, immediate or remote, is there lhat'Mr. Edwar'd
Martyn’s section will do anything effective? And if
both keep on slinging adjectives at each other they need
not think that the country will get excited ; the country
will merely say a plague on both your houses. T!m
Parliamentary Party failed badly over the Councils
Bill, but when John Redmond flopped he fell on top of
Edward Martyn.

In our issue of May 25th we said:—‘‘ It seems to us
that if the Bill is kicked out by the Convention that
the Parliamentary Party, as a leading national force,
gets its quictus by the same kick.”” And that is pre-
cisely wkat has happened. We laugh at the hysterics
of Mr. Martyn’s Party, but the Irish Parliamentary
Party has ceased to be a leading national force. ~We
think a party in Parliament, or a party thatare ready
to go to Parliament when called upon as a fire brigade
is ready to gallop out when the fire alarm rings, is a
necessary part of the Irish machinery ; but the Party, as
a leading national force, got its quietus when Mr. Red-
mond moved the rejection of the Irish Councils Bill.
We do not tkink that Mr. Redmond and his advisers,
and the ““ Freeman’s Journal,”” appreciate the realities
of the situation they have created ; the stage is at pre-
sent more or less clear for the antics of the Martynettes.
but the future holds a new combination and a new line
of tactics, and Mr. Martyn’s future is already behind
Lim. s

Political matters will settle down in due course, and
many pecple of power are, no doubt, at the present
moment centen’ + [ cboklz tho Lest
and most useful thing they could do. A cool, masterful
business man does not feel inclined to speak to. a cus-
tomer who is labouring under excitement, he would
prefer.to put off the interview with the customer until
the customer regains his normal coolness. The present
Parliamentary Party, backed up by the ‘“ Freeman’s
Jeurnal,”” have failed, and failed miserably, and they
will have to alter their procedure and their tactics.
They will have to do more: they will have to take a
back seat in the National hall. Tke Martynette revo-
lutionary party is comic, and. will fizzle out’; the fubure
is not for them. But while sensible people laugh at
the Kildare Street oxtremist,” it is well t]m('-athey
should not lose sight of the rather hopeless state of the

Freeman’s Journal ”’ and its Parliamentary Party.

In the futu're the centre of Irish national gravity will
tend to swing from the rural to tke urban centres.
Rural Treland has kept Ireland playing its political
tune for a long time, and rural Ireland has got its
various Land Acts, and it is now for it to make the best
of them, a_mltgr and amend them where desirable, but,
above all, it is for rural Ireland to tackle to its business
as a business, and learn all that is to be learned about
mnden.\ and scientific farming, co-operate where co-
operation is beneficial, and generally run the farming
industry and business on up-to-date lines. The politi-
cal future is comparatively with the urban centres and
the manufacturers’ interests; .we say comparatively,
for up to this the rural men called nearl y all the tune
and the interests of Trish business men and manufac-
turers were not a big, or scarcely any, element in the
national political world of Treland. If the manufac-
turmg and business elements are to be interested in an
organised national movement the ‘“ Freeman’s Journal’’
must take a back seat, and the United Irish League
must be radically altered, and the Parliamentary Party
must not only be changed in personnel, but as a Par-
liamentary Party it must reconcile itself to become a
cog-wheel, and nothing more, in the Frish national
mackine.

The sensible business people of urban Ireland must
have an influence commensurate with their power and
importance in organised national affairs, and if these
are to come in the ‘ extremist’’ of Kildare St. Club,
and such men, will have to quickly step down. A
movement that anathematises a man because he is a Jus-
tice of the Peace will not get any countenance from
level-headed business men and manufacturers. The
national organisation of the near future must be based
partly on conciliation—but conciliation and concilia-
tion gone mad are different things. We are interested
observers of the present whirligig, and we look compla-
cently at it. We know our own know, and will stop
Eere for the present.

His Grace the Archbishop of Tuam, in the course of
his remarks on the occasion of the opening of the Con-
nacht Feis, said:—‘“ Of course, in Ireland our prin-
cipal industry is agriculture, and Irish agriculture is

throughout a great part of Ireland, especially in the
West, in a deplorably backward state. This is mainly
due to the fact that in the rural primary schools no
provision whatsoever has been made by the Commis-
sioners of National Education for teaching even the
rudiments of practical agriculture, although an agricul-
tural class-book would be also a useful literary class-
book. And yet, two things are undeniable; first, that
ninc-tenths of the pupils in rural schools can never go
beyond the National School, and, consequently, never
geb an opportunity of learning elsewhere the better
way of doing that which is to be the main business of
their after life. Ilence it is that agriculture is in a
more backward condition in Ireland than, perhaps, in
any other country in Europe, and that state of things
is sure to continue until adequate provision is made for
teaching practical agriculture in our primary schools.”

This was not the first time that His Grace pleaded for
agricultural education in the primary schools. Every
rural primary school in the country should have an
agricultural plot attached to it, and it were about time
that public opinion became articulate on the matter.
Perhaps, General Sir William Butler, the latest addi-
tion to the ranks of the Commissioners of National
Education, would press this matter on the Commis-
sioners. We feel sure that any matter that he would
attempt to press would at least be forcibly pressed.

If education in Ireland were headed in a useful and
healthy direction this country has splendid educational
machinery. The Colleges and convents are in a position
to give effective education at a comparatively small
cost; and if the ‘“ Intermediate ’’ system would let them
we are sure the nuns would rather be turning out girls
fit to be sensible wives than mere stall-fed Intermediate
result earners that are fit to fill positions that do not
exist and unfit for stations in life that cry in vain for
people to fill them. In these days of political twaddle
one sees in an extra clear light how sadly the country
is handicapped and muddled because of the results of
the non-education and mis-education of the people.

meeting in Tara Hall. It was the occasion of a lecture
by Mx. Joseph Devlin, M.P:. We read in"thoreport in
Mr., ‘William Murphy’s ‘ Nationalist organ.’ /that—
‘The speaker. (Skeffy) wished. to deprecate the fact of
running the risk of an open.breach with the Liberal
Party, and the surrender to the arrogant type of clergy-
men, who, he said, were never satisfied unless they had
the laity under their feet.”” This tilting at the ‘‘arro-
gant type of clergymen ’’ did not agree evidently. with
the humour of the meeting. There were cries of ‘‘ Sit
down’” and ‘‘ Withdraw ’’ and ‘‘ Shut up.” Skelly,
clad in the mantle of Michael Davitt, appears to have
drawn in his horns promptly. That is a rather mixed
sort of a picture; but a hyphened picture admirably
suits a hyphened democrat. Skeffy is reported to have
expressed sorrow if he had touched on any objectionable
matters! What a robust democrat, to be sure—if you
don’t like my views I'm sorry I mentioned them!

A contributor writes: —‘“ A trip from the hot city
to Blackrock in ideal summer weather is a most enjoy-
able experience even on an ordinary occasion. Many
Irish-Irelanders had, on Saturday last, an additional
pleasure in the journey. Their destination was the
Frankfort Feis. En Route the figure of Fionan
MacColuim was sufficient information that something
or other concerned with Irish-Ireland was near at hand,
and on entering the Feis ground the first personality
to catch our eyes was Father Little, P.P., from Co.
Clare—fitting introduction to a gathering of the kind.
The  green sward, happy faces, bright dresses, white
marquees, blue sky and shading trees, made a pleasant
picture that sent the fingers of a photographic friend
accompanying us instinctively to his kodak. The Frank-
fort. Feis was instituted three years ago, and it has been
held annually since. From the point of view of entries
and attendance I was glad to learn that this year’s was
a distinct success. ~Were I corresponding with a
“society ’ journal I might say that the attendance was
‘large and fashionable.” But seriously it struck me
as a large and well-dressed audience taking a keen in-
terest in the proceedings, and an audience, too, amongst
which one heard the Irish language spoken pretty
freely. So far as the competitors are concerned it was
a children’s Feis. To make the children Irish is a

" necessity that impresses us all, and the Frankfort Feis
is an encouraging illustration of how to try to do it.

As announced in our advertising columns, a public
meeting will be held on next Sunday the 28th inst., at
one o’clock sharp in Bray, at which His Grace the
Archbishop of Dublin will preside. The debt on the
parish is a very heavy one, being upwards of nine
thousand pounds. The churches in Bray and Grey-
stones on which this debt has been incurred are excel-
lent specimens of ecclesiastical art. The meeting has
been called to raise funds to clear off portion of this
heavy liability. The object has our warmest sympathy
and we strongly urge all classes of the community to
give generously on the occasion:

On Sunday next an open-air cntertainment will be
held in the grounds of {he Capuchin’s Franciscan
Monastery, Rochestown, in aid of the funds of the New
College. = Rochestown College has a big name in_the
Irish-Treland educational world. Besides songs there
will be Irish dances, Irish pipes, the Cork working-
men’s prize band, the South Parish Gaelic League Choir
and the Blackpool Gaelic League orchestra will be there.
Father Augustine and others will speak, and refresh-
ments can be had on the grounds. There will be a cheap
excursion from Cork, and motor cars will ply between
Rochestown Station and the Monastery. We hope that
the Gaelic Leaguers of Cork and their friends will rally
on this occasion to the support of Rochestown College.

The Skibbereen Gas Company advertised a few weeks,
ago for a gas manager, although having a thoroughly
competent man on the spot who was sent on by the
Cork Gas Co. The.Secretary, we hear, wrote to two
English firms to send on a man, and each of them did
s0. The first Englishman who arrived got the situa-
tion. He was a gas fitter from Longwood, Yorkshire.
Perhaps one should not be surprised that a small town
like Skibbereen, whose ‘‘eagle’’ has its eye on the
Emperor of Russia, should go to England for its
gas manager. Gas works in Cork, Dungarvan, Wex-
ford, Tralee and other places are managed by Irishmen,
and managed successfully. How would the Secretary
in Skibbereen like it if ‘‘ No Irish need apply ” was
adopted by the Dingle Railway Company when the
latter wanted an engineer?

THE DEPARTMENT IN BEING.

IV.—A Trrp To DARRARA.

E venture to say that few readers of our metro-

politan daily papers have even heard of Darrara
—or Ta-ra-ra as a friend of ours, in a flippant moment,
styled it. It is not in touch with the various British
Press agencies that supply our so-called Irish National
dailies with their news; it is a cypher compared with
the Thaw case or the wrestlers at the music halls. And
yet Darrara is an important fact in the slow and sure
revolution that is working its way in Ireland more in-
sistently and more vigorously than most people are
aware.

You could see the Atlantic ocean glistening in the
scorching sunlight on the 17th July. Darrara is some-
where about 250 feet above the sea level, and its 340
acres are situate in a grand piece of country, three miles
or so from the town of Clonakilty, famous for its Rast-
ler’” and cursed by its “Pride of race.”’ Clonakilty has
its thriving-looking side, but it is for the most part, if
we may judge from the glimpse, we got of it, as ram-
shackle looking an old town as we have seen for a long
time. There is, so far as we know, no industry in the
town (except the “ Rastler’’), though the "rise of
Darrara and the early potato growing in the district
may help to shake it up.

Thoro were about 200 farmers at Darrara on the
17th. And such farmers. They were a body of met
calculated to make one optimistic. Probably they wers
all, or nearly all, what are called strong *’ farmers,
and it may be argued that the people who want in-
struction most are the ““ small’’ farmers. We are not
going to dogmatise on the matter, but we recall the
dictum of Mr. Forth, of the Belfast Technical Institute,
that those who win Technical scholarships need them
most, and similarly we are inclined to think that those
farmers who go and take the information offered are
those whom it will benefit most. Most of the 200 farmers

338

had to go to considerable trouble and some expense to
attend Darrara, and their day away from their farms and
the sun shining was no doubt a considerable matter for
many of them. They went there for information, and
any information that was to be picked up those shrewd
men picked it up; probably some of them thought, not
without reason, that they could give a losson or two
even to Darrara. The principal of the school, whose
accent, as well as his name, suggests his _Scol.gh
nationality, delivered a- series of loctures or talks to
the' farmers in meeting assembled. The farmers lis-
tened attentively and eagerly. Nccdl'cs.s to say, Ll'm"f
was no cheering, the farmers \\'Cl‘clCI'l(-lCﬂ”y sifting or
quietly absorbing the information given by I\L([‘r, l)uncmz;
We were glad to note that Mr. Duncan was heckled.
There wore some stalks of barley amongst a field of oats,
and one of the farmers, with a suggestion of Sl!b-«'lcl([
humour, wanted to know the reason why ¢ Faith, he
hasn’t the thistles cut himself,”” said another farmer
in our hearing as we walked a field where sheep were
grazing. Some one officially, or unofficially, offered an
explanation. We only refer to the remark by way of
illustrating the critical attitude of at least some of 15113
farmers. But we do not wish to convey the impression
that the farmers were in a hypercritical mood, they were
there for information, in the best of humour, and the
friendliest of disposition. Mr. Duncan, the principal at
Darrara, is a plain man with no nonsense about him.
He had made many practical experiments recently, and
he talked to the farmers on the results. — This is a
general article for general readers and they do not
want to read minute details of the value of certain
manures to certain crops, and if they did we would
have to get Mr. Duncan to write the article, for though
we heard him, our agricultural education is of a very
unspecialised kind. But the results of these various
trials and experiments have a direct bread and butter
value to the farmers who visited Darrara and to those
who will read the official reports of these trials when
{ they are published. Mr. Duncan carried out experi-
ments of keeping cattle for winter dairying. Winter
dairying, we gather from those who ought to know,
is a matter of vast importance to the country; it not
only means millions to it, but if it is not developed it
is not at all certain that the Irish butter industry can
even keep marking time. The experiments in this
b matter worked out at a financial profit; but winter
| fattening of cattle at Darrara, when the two sides of
{ the account were totted up, showed a balance on the
| wrong side. The farmers followed all {hese discourses
i with the greatest interest and asked several questions.
It was a new order of speechifying to them. A man
on top of a ‘“ditch,”” holding forth to a meeting of
farmers on the pros and cons of cow-feeding and pig-
feeding, is a picture of the new order. The old order
was a fierce struggle for a footing on the land, the new
order is a desire for development in the methods for
taking the highest possible produce out of the land ; and
with a change of orders there is naturally a change in
the style of oratory, and the ‘“loud and prolonged
cheering >’ listeners give way fto calculators and
““ hecklers.”’

There is a stallion at Darrara, half brother to Orby
who won the Derby, and a great big bony Spanish jack-
ass brought over with a view of improving the breed
of the patient and long-suffering Irish apat.  Certainly
progress is in the air when the Irish ass is to undergo
improvement ! )

The excursion of inspection to Darrara was organised
by Mr. J. T. Carroll, the pushful secretary of the
Cork County Committee of Agriculture, a level-headed
enthusiast, who farms in a large way himself up one of
the slopes of the happy valley within car-shot almost
of the hum of industry at Dripsey’s now famous wool-
len mills. An excursion like that is a large order in
the organising way, it involved a special train, catering,
and a hundred other details, but all went off without a
hitch. Everyonoc was pleased, and Mr. Carroll went
for a swim the next morning at seven o’clock as if he
had only done an ordinary day’s work the day before.

Ladies graced the visit to Darrara by their presence,

In the railway carriage down we were vis-a-vis with
Mr. Williamson, of Mallow, the famous potato
specialist, and we talked potatoes half the way, from
Cork to Clonakilty. We had never dreamt that pota-
toes were such an interesting subject. A lady entered N
the compartment at one of the stations down the line,
and no sooner were we introduced to her than potatoes
had to be dropped, as if they”were hot, and we were
put on our defence. Alas, we cannot go anywhere but
we are fated to meet people we have “ gone”” for. It
appears that in' the dim and distant past, when some
schools were not as good as they have since become, we
gave a few stinging paragraphs to an entertainment in
a certain convent school at which this lady was then
a brilliant and distinguished pupil. She recited on
the occasion. She and several of her schoolfellows
could have torn us to pieces had they caught us whilst
they were hot over the innocent paragraphs. The
convent school is very much improved since these para-
graphs appeared, and here was one of its past students
going to Darrara! We did not rub in the moral to
be drawn from these facts as we might have done. We
took our punishment in the most good-humoured and
becoming disposition, and in less than two minutes we
were the best of friends. If all those concerning whom
we have at one time or another found it our duty to say
severe things only knew the serene spirit with which we
do it for their own good, they would pray for us rather
than desire to tear us to pieces |

Darrara is a school as well as a place for experiment-
ing in the breeding and feeding of live-stock, poultry,
cte., tillage, dairying, and so forth. Apprentices to
the farming industry are taken in residence for an
eleven months’ term, and it is an eleven months’ system
that can be well recommended.  Whilst old farmers,
with their prejudices and crusted habits, stand to learn
much from the experiments carried on at the farm, we
suppose, as in the language movement, most hope is set
in the schools and the young generation. Certainly
farmers need not complain on the ground of expense if
they wish to send their sons for a session to Darrara.
Farmers of a valuation that does not exceed :£20 need
only pay £3 each for the eleven-month session at
the College for their sons ; not exceeding £40 valuation,
£6; not exceeding £100 valuation, £10; over £100
valuation, £15, and the fee for apprentices outside
these classes, £20 a year. Certainly no one can cavil
at these terms. Bed and board and apprenticed for
eleven months to a profession under highly specialised
instruction for £3! An intending apprentice must, and
very properly too, give an undertaking that he intends
to become a farmer in Ireland, and he must provide
evidence that he has a sure prospect of obtaining a
farm of his own or bona-fide occupation on a farm.
The apprentices reside in the building attached to the
station.  We had a peep at the dormitory and so forth,
and certainly the place is excellently appointed. There
are 25 students at Darrara, and the year, or session,
commences early in October and ends early in Septem-
ber. It is needless to say much in the way of detail
about the school. Each pupil has a plot all to himself,

BEAUTY AND IRELAND. 
W-
HAT a splendid captivity is ours in Ireland. 
How glorious the island home, that is our 
prison. 
And what a wonderland that wesLern shore, 
that is, as Paul Dubois points out, the setting to so 
much wretchedness and misery, a gaol with bars of 
gold. 
I have seen most of the beauty spots of Europe, 
and you shall find in other places a rugged grandeur 
that is not in our island. 
But for beauty, pure and 
godlike, lustrous as of jewels, the colour symphony of 
a setting sun, our soil is blessed beyond others. 
\.Valk 
out from the Claddagh, with its desolate congeries of 
mean huts, with its slattern children, traitor . to their 
mother tongue; pass these things by and look across the 
bay. 
Hea,vens, what magnificence! 
The darker blue 
near the shore, with the coloured sail for a foil, the 
brighter band of opal out beyond, stretchi11g to the 
purple hills of the other shore; out to the west the 
changing hues of the evening sky, all open grand, im-
measured in its soft expanse, with the soughing of the 
waves upon the shore for its accompaniment. 
Or stand 
upon some Kerry hill, and see the mountains, softly 
gentle in their forms, of varied purple in their shades, 
set off against the bright glistening, vivid blue of the 
bay, and the unsullied green of the foliage, vaulted 
with a grey-blue sky of infinite depth. 
How weak are 
words to set down their loveliness, to describe the sweet 
joy of the soul that takes bi ·th from scenes so beauti-
ful. 
Stresa and Maggiore • are the pride of Italy. 
Glengariff is not one whit less lovely. 
· 
No land has received a greater heritage of natural 
beauty than our own. 
Yet it is a> cloistered beauty; for 
t.he world knows it not. 
The beauty is part of our 
1ives ; it is all around us; can we transfer it to our 
handiwork, our acts. 
Why has no great world painter 
arisen to record upon his canvas that 'splendour that is 
around us? 
Were not worthy furniture fit for this~ our 
old beautiful home 1 Could we not 1:,ut into plastic 
matter, something of the grand reality of sky, and sea 
and mountain in which . we live 7 If Italy were made 
by nature to be land of art and song, a land that is 
·itself a poem, is Ireland a land less fit for art 7 But 
-the exceilencies are wont to dwell together. 
With the 
decay of spirit, art has passed from Italy across the 
Alps. 
And as the nature of Ireland h?s decayerl and 
its spirit slumbered, art, too, has penshed, and our 
beautiful hills are dwelt upon by men with vacant 
minds and unskilled hands.

beautiful 
intricacies of

sents and the like. 
Were it not better than to buy 
t;he truth and beauty th~.t are Ireland's than the o-litter-
ing ugliness of the 'Grafton Street shop 1 N othi~g can

It should be remembered that scientific Domestic
Economy is not merely limited to strictly handi-
craft matters, such as cookery, laundrying, needle-

in.  Any sort of a real working knowledge of the

to administer and lay out to the best possible advantage

usually put in the hands of the women of the family.
In the continental regular schools of housewifery, which
were the first established and have been in existence for
generations in sound working order, this question of
competency in marketing gets the foremost place—in-
stead of the hindmost, as in this country—when here
it happens to be thought of at all. In those continen-
tal housewifery schools it is recognised as the main-
spring of all the practical issues. Its bearings are con-
sidered—and taught to be considered—in respect of im-
mediate domestic, extending to local, and from that up
to national, interests. Whereas in this country, young
girls with domestic tastes and not a few domestic
accomplishments are often found simply witless in
setting about buying even the most trifling commodity
in a shop. ‘ Mother does it!”’ Young girls brought
up in this manner are, as a rule, absolutely ignorant
of,-and indifferent to, what can be done by the women
of Ireland, who have a goodwill to help on the Irish
Tndustrial Revival. And they cannot be blamed for
it: because of their upbringing.

HOME INDUSTRIES AT THE EXHIBITION.

A VISIT to this section emphasises the absence of

orderly arrangement. It is my first experience
of an International Exhibition, and my view may,
therefore, be in conflict with precedent, but it occurs
to me that a coherent disposition of the goods of ecach
country represented should have been an important
objective of the organising authorities. The presence
of a Canadian, a French, a Home Industries depart-
ment, etc., suggests that something like this was in
their minds originally. But if so, it has unfortunately
not been very successfully developed, and the Irish
exhibits apparently are the greatest sufferers thercby.
T have heard the Home Industries Section referred to
as the ‘“ Irish Section,’”’ as if everything that Ireland
had on show might be expected to be found in it, and
many people like the King, with little time to throw
away, are satisfied with a glance around it for their
impressions of Irish industries. There is undoubtedly
a lot to be looked at in this section, but properly spealk-
ing it should only be a part of a whole which ought to
include all our Industrial products on view. It would
then be possible to make a connected inspection of them
in a reasonable time. At present you stumble across
them here, there, almost everywhere, and in more or
less unexpected places, and their confusing disarrange-
ment forcibly suggests to you the truth of the saying:
““ Union is strength.””

In justice to the organisers, however, it should be
=2id that there may have been technical and financial
«difficulties in the way, one might not expect perhaps
that a machinery hall should be fixed up for each
country and so on. But taking even the present system
as a basis, matters could have been much improved by
2 better distribution of exhibits.

Besides informing us of many industrial activities in
Treland at present, the Home Industries Section fur-
nishes illustrations of many industries that are not,
but might be, carried on here—and some stands are ex-
clusively devoted to this educational purpose.  The
possible utilization of Irish hides, skins, bones, hair
and fur by exhibits of articles imported, but made from
Irish raw material. Real TIrish kid skin gloves are
shown here, made in England, and underncath them
a pair of gloves of Limerick make, but unfortunately so
far back as the early part of the 19th century when that
city was a seat of glove manufacture. g

The Limerick gloves on show are so cxceptionally
fine that they fit into a walnut shell. -

By the way I was glad to notice an exhibit which
gives hope fhat this manufacture may be restored to
Ireland.  Several beautiful specimens stitched by Tip-
perary girls are displayed at the stand of Messrs.
Fownes, the big English makers, who recently estab-
lished the industry in Tipperary. On inquiryI learned
that about 30 girls are already employed. So far I
helieve the gloves are only stitched here. It occurred
to me that the cutfing-out might also be done at Tip-
perary, but this part I understand is the work of men
and has not yet been introduced. As we are badly in
need of opportunities for male labour it is to be hoped
Messrs. Townes will soon see their way clear for further
developments. Messrs. Mitchell & Son have already
beon mentioned in the LEADER as the only Irish manu-
facturers of combs. Unfortunately, however, their out-
put is but a very small fraction of the total Irish
demand for these articles. A stand showing varieties
of combs and other goods made from horn, hoof and
bone is a gloomy comment on our huge importations in
this line. An exhibit of what Ireland makes and
should make from her clays recalled the reference in
last week’s LEADER to Cornish enterprise in cornering
a rich deposit of china clay somewhere in Antrim, which
will probably be shipped to England and turned into
porcelain there. ~ Various examples of pottery are
shown, made by a Birmingham firm out of Irish clay.
The exhibits of Belleek pottery, however, bore pleas-
ing testimony that some people in Ireland recognise that
growing potatoes and cabbage, etc., is not tke only use
that can be made of Irish soil. Articles made in the home
in other countries, at present imported into Ireland, are
represented by artificial flowers, feather boas, etc.,
which it is suggested might possibly afford similar
employment in this country. The Tobacco Growing
Association includes, I notice, the following counties:
—DMeath, Louth, Kilkenny, Wexford, and King’s Co.
This is a most interesting exhibit, the little model of a
tobacco plantation looks sufficiently realistic to inspire
an author of coon melodies.

Now that cutlery manufacture has been established
in Dublin, a stand containing cutlery draws special
altention. On one side of the stand of a Dublin firm
I noticed the announcement—*‘ Dublin cutlery made a
hundred years ago,’”’ and the display it refers to, in-
cluding razors, penknives, scissors, carvers, table knives,
ete., is surprising evidence of how fast modern Dublin
will have to travel to make up for lost ground in this
industry. On another side of the stand under notice
a collection of imported cutlery is shown bearing an
announcement to that effect.

There is a numerous and varied display of lace, wool-
len, carpet weaving and other industries. Unfortu-
nately my visit was paid during the tea hour, and I
consequently missed seeing many of these exhibits in
the work of production. The dulness of rural life is a
favourite explanation of Ireland’s emigration figures,
and one would naturally like to see as many Irish vil-
lage halls as there are villages, but one would like,
however, also to see them of Irish manufacture as far
as nessible.  The medel village hall at Herbert Park
is the work of Humphreys and Co., and would probably
h{:}d between two and three hundred people comfort-
ably.

Village hospitals are as much a necessity in their
own way as village halls. The example on exhibition
is of neat construction, and apparently well equipped,
but it strikes a non-expert as being rather of the doll-
house size. ITowever, a small one is better than none
ab all, and probably a great many Irish villages remote
too from hospital centres, are without anything worthy
of the name.

HEN I begin to think of what I haYe to say 
about the Dubliner, two questions claim answers: 
Vlhere did you meet him_? 
How long do you know 
him? 
Well, I met him iu a casual way, in streets, 
shops, restam·auts, but mostly on tram tops. 
I do not 
say I studied him! I never interviewed him; I took no 
notes. 
As for the second question, I have to say that 
I spent three periods in Dublin, each separ~ted by some 
years from the others. 
Now, deary Dubliner, whatever 
I have to say to you, take it with these two questions 
in your mind, and their answers. 
I conceive the Dubliner to be very quiet, very honest, 
rather · innocent (when compared especially with the 
people of small towns), light-headed person. There, 
it's a.JI out together! 
Now, I won't attempt to prove all these charges-
that the Dubliner is honest, who doubts? That he is 
quiet, whoever heard him making a row? 
That he is 
innocent, I think his commercial methods show. 
He 
is usually claimed as humorous, but I cannot say that 
I heard much humour in Dublin-I remember noting 
t.hat the illustrations in the Leprachcmn were better 
than the matter. But one bit of Dublin humour comes 
back. 
A poor withered old fellow got into a rather 
full tram, and sat next the door. 
He was the last per-
son upon whom a crowd would turn their eyes. 
Yet 
that came about. He brought it about himself, for 
upon anyone getting out the old fellow instantly occu-
pied the vacated space. 
In this way he made a sort 
of zig-zag progress from the door right up to the piano 
seat in the centre. 
We all thought that that proud 
eminence would satisfy him. 
But no, he was not satis-
fied. 
Presently the man sitting at his side, in the 
corner, got up. 
The old fellow literally jumped down 
into the corner, put his. feet v.ncler the "piano" stool 
and beamed-not on us, but on his feet, now curled 
round the spindle. 
Then he quietly broke the ominous 
silence, still beaming on his foot-wear-" There y'are-
not a bit o' fear of ye." He raised his head-"Corns," 
he said, explainingly. 
Another very old fellow I 
heard shouting at a hurling match in Jones's Road-
" Go on, me ould warryor," but tho humour in this 
case was in the broken-backed, broken-winded appear-
ance of the enthusiast. 
On the whole the people in 
Dublin who go tram-riding do not seem to get much 
humour out of life.

One scorching day I had lunch in one of the

I felt truly miserable,

keeper in Dublin.

begin to wonder if any customers went away un-

One last trifle and we will leave our peace with the

at least know their business failings ; I had rather they 
did not.

like." I felt awfully weak, quite faint, iu fact, at this

We do not know in what business Mr. Donohoe, J.P.,
serves himself and his country, but part of our business,
as responsible journalists, is to stick out for a meaning
for words. Commenting on his letter, we pointed .out
that the word ‘‘ powerless’’ was an exaggeration, and
heaven knows Ireland has suffered so much from inflated
language and want of perspicuity that it is a wise and
wholesome thing to draw attention to the want of them.
Mr. James Donohoe, although he is a J.P., wrote a
letter to the Martynette meeting at Enniscorthy, and
J.P.’s, if anything, are worse than policemen in the
rebellious eyes of the party presided over by the Kil-
dare Street Club ‘“rebel.” We still think that it is
an exaggeration to call the Irish Party powerless. One
instance will prove our contention. = We fought the
bigotry of the Great Northern Railway, and worked
up public opinion on it. Mr. Michael Sullivan and
others did a lot of work with a view to establishing some
sort of competitive examination for the clerkships of the
Company. Our efforts and Mr. Sullivan’s efforts,
though effective in operating on public opinion, were
not sufficient to compel the directors of the Great
Northern Railway to throw open their clerkships to
competition. ~ Members of the Irish Parliamentary
Party blocked a Bill of the Great Northern Railway in
Parliament, and after a struggle the Great Northern
Railway directors were brought to a fairly reasonable
state of mind by the dynamic force exercised in Parlia-
ment, and they established partially competitive exam-
inations. We will go no further than this case, for it
serves our purpose; it logically proves that ifi is an
exaggeration to say that the Irish Party are powerless.
